---
title: "Week 3: Intro to `{purrr}`"
subtitle: "& Lab 2"
author: "Joe Nese"
institute: "University of Oregon<br>Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: default
    highlight-style: github
    incremental: false
#    fig-width: 9
#    fig-height: 3.5
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
execute: 
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

```{r}
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
theme_set(theme_minimal(14))
```

# `{purrr}` & Lab 2

**Week 3**

## Agenda {.smaller}

* Review Lab 1
* Thinking more about functional programming
    + A small example
* Introduce you to `{purrr}` and contrast it with the base functions we learned last week

**Learning objectives**

* Understand how `purrr::map()` relates to `lapply()` and `for` loops
* Understand the four basic variants of `purrr::map()`, when they should be used, and when they will fail
* Understand what functional programming is, and how `{purrr}` can help facilitate the process

# Review Lab 1

# Functional Programming

## What is Functional Programming?

> decomposing a big problem into smaller pieces, then solving each piece with a function or combination of functions <br> \-- [Adv-R](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/fp.html)

## Example

Calculate top mpg manufactures

(let's just subset the data to 4 cylinders for this example)


```{r four_cyl}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
four_cyl <- filter(mpg, cyl == 4)
```

## Example

Now we'll filter for cases where the city miles per gallon is in the top 10% (i.e., greater than or equal to the $90^{th}$ percentile)

```{r ninety-ptile}
#| output-location: fragment
ninety <- four_cyl |>
	filter(cty >= quantile(cty, probs = 0.9))
ninety
```

## Example

Now let's count the unique occurrences for each manufacturer, manufacturer/model, and class

```{r counts}
(count_mfr <- count(ninety, manufacturer))
(count_model <- count(ninety, manufacturer, model))
(count_class <- count(ninety, class))
```

## Example

Produce a plot for each

```{r plots1}
plot_mfr <- count_mfr |> 
	ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(manufacturer, -n), n)) +
		geom_col(aes(fill = manufacturer)) +
		scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
		labs(title = "Manufacturers",
		     x = "",
		     y = "") +
		guides(fill = "none")
plot_mfr
```

## 

```{r}
plot_car <- count_model |>
	unite(car, manufacturer, model, sep = " ") |>
	ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(car, -n), n)) +
		geom_col(aes(fill = car)) +
		scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
		labs(title = "Top 10% of city mpg",
		     subtitle = "Car frequency",
		     x = "",
		     y = "") +
		guides(fill = "none")
plot_car
```

## 

```{r}
plot_class <- count_class |> 
  ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(class, -n), n)) +
		geom_col(aes(fill = class)) +
		scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
		labs(title = "Car Class",
		     x = "", 
		     y = "") +
		guides(fill = "none")
plot_class
```


## Assemble the plots

```{r patchwork}
library(patchwork)
plot_car / (plot_mfr + plot_class)
```

# Functional Programming Version

At least in spirit

## Let's Expand from 4 cyl

We did a trial run with one piece (4 cyl), now let's do some functional programming and apply it to all `cyl` groups

```{r}
by_cyl <- split(mpg, mpg$cyl)
```

. . .

And filter all

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
top_10 <- lapply(by_cyl, function(x) {
	filter(x, cty >= quantile(cty, probs = 0.9))
})
str(top_10)
```

## Counts for all

More complex anonymous function here

```{r all-counts}
#| output-location: slide
counts <- lapply(top_10, function(x) {
	
  count_manufacturer <- count(x, manufacturer)
	
  count_class <- count(x, class)
  
	count_model <- count(x, manufacturer, model) |>
		unite(car, manufacturer, model, sep = " ")
	
  
	return(list(mfr = count_manufacturer, 
	            car = count_model, 
	            class = count_class))
})
counts
```

## Plots for all {.smaller}

Let's write a couple functions

(we'll mostly ignore how for now)

```{r}
counts_plot <- function(counts_df) {
  
  var <- names(counts_df)[1]
	
  p <- ggplot(counts_df, aes(fct_reorder(!!sym(var), -n), n)) +
		geom_col(aes(fill = !!sym(var))) +
		scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
		labs(title = stringr::str_to_title(var),
		     x = "",
		     y = "") +
		guides(fill = "none") +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

	if(var == "car") {
		p <- p + labs(title = "Top 10% of city mpg",
		              subtitle = var)
	}
 p
}
```


## Test it "4"

```{r}
counts_plot(counts[["4"]]$mfr)
```	

## Test it "6"

```{r indiv_plot3, fig.width = 13}
counts_plot(counts[["6"]]$class)	
```

## Test it "8"

```{r indiv_plot2, fig.width = 13}
counts_plot(counts[["8"]]$car)
```

## Compile plots function

```{r plot2}
full_plot <- function(l) {
	counts_plot(l[["car"]]) / (
	  counts_plot(l[["mfr"]]) +
	  counts_plot(l[["class"]])
	)
}
```

## Test it

```{r full_plot-test, fig.width = 13}
#| output-location: fragment
full_plot(counts[[1]])
```

## Finish up

```{r looped-full-plot}
plots <- lapply(counts, full_plot)
```

## 4 cyl (1)

```{r p1}
#| output-location: fragment
plots[[1]]
```

## 5 cyl (2)

```{r p2}
#| output-location: fragment
plots[[2]]
```

## 6 cyl (3)

```{r p3}
#| output-location: fragment
plots[[3]]
```

## 8 cyl (4)

```{r p4}
plots[[4]]
```

# `{purrr}`

## Functionals

**functional**
: "a function that takes a function as input and returns a vector as output"

. . .

You remember from last week and `lapply()` et al.

## `purrr::map()`

```{r map}
library(purrr) # loaded automatically with library(tidyverse)
map(1:3, rnorm)
```


## Graphically

![](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/f0494d020aa517ae7b1011cea4c4a9f21702df8b/2577b/diagrams/functionals/map.png)

## Comparison to `base::lapply()`

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`lapply()`**
```{r lapply-map}
lapply(1:3, rnorm)
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**`map()`**
```{r map-lapply}
map(1:3, rnorm)
```
:::

::::

::: aside
side note: What exactly is going on here?
:::

## `map()` & `lapply()`

> The base equivalent to `map()` is `lapply()`. The only difference is that `lapply()` does not support the helpers that you’ll learn about (next), so if you’re only using `map()` from `{purrr}`, you can skip the additional dependency and use `lapply()` directly.

\- [Adv R](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/functionals.html)

## Equivalents

The following are equivalent

```{r equivalents1}
#| eval: false
map(mtcars, function(x) length(unique(x)))

lapply(mtcars, function(x) length(unique(x)))
```

## EXPLAIN .X

## So why {.smaller}

But `{purrr}` also allows  you to specify anonymous functions more succinctly using the formula interface

. . .

From this:

```{r}
#| eval: false
map(mtcars, function(x) length(unique(x)))
```

To this:

```{r formula1}
map(mtcars, ~length(unique(.x)))
```

. . .

If you forget the `~` (I do sometimes!)

```{r}
#| error: true
map(mtcars, length(unique(.x)))
```

## Anonymous function?

- An anonymous function is one that is defined without being given a name
- Since it has no name assigned with `<-`, it’s anonymous
- It’s usually created for short, one-time use—often inside another function like `lapply()`, `sapply()`, `purrr::map()`, etc.

```{r}
#| eval: false
function(arguments) {
  # code
}
```


# `~` is used in place of `function(x)`

## More examples {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
Vary the `n`
```{r }
map(1:3, ~rnorm(n = .x))
```
:::

::: {.column width="34%"}
Vary the `mean`
```{r }
map(1:3, ~rnorm(n = 1, mean = .x))
```
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
Vary the `sd`
```{r }
map(1:3, ~rnorm(n = 1, sd = .x))
```
:::

::::

## Remember `lapply()` {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}

This doesn't work

```{r}
#| error: true
lapply(1:3, rnorm(n = x))
```

:::

::: {.column width="34%"}

or this

```{r}
#| error: true
lapply(1:3, ~rnorm(n = x))
```
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}

It has to be this

```{r}
lapply(1:3, function(x) rnorm(n = x))
```

:::

::::

## Extracting elements

Let's make a complicated list

```{r list}
(l <- list(
  list(-1, x = 1, y = 2, z = "a"),
  list(-2, x = 4, y = c(5, 6), z = "b"),
  list(-3, x = 8, y = c(9, 10, 11))
))
```

## Extract second element from each

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}

`map()` 😎

```{r map-extract}
map(l, 2)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}

`lapply` 👎

```{r lapply-extract-fail}
#| error: true
lapply(l, 2)
```

:::

::::

## Doesn't work with `lapply()`

Instead, you have to apply an anonymous function

```{r lapply-extract-success1}
lapply(l, function(x) x[[2]])
```

. . .

Alternatively the following is also the same (and my brain hates it)

```{r lapply-extract-success2}
#| eval: false
lapply(l, `[[`, 2)
```

## Extract by name

```{r by_name}
map(l, "y")
```

## Multiple arguments

Extract first element `1` from `y`

```{r by_name_arg}
map(l, list("y", 1))
```

# `{purrr}` variants

## Return a vector

And specify the type

* `map_dbl()`
* `map_int()`
* `map_chr()`
* `map_lgl()`

## `l`

```{r}
str(l)
```

## `map_*`

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%" .fragment}

```{r}
map(l, "x")
```

:::

::: {.column width="34%" .fragment}
```{r show-l}
map_dbl(l, "x")
```
:::

::: {.column width="33%" .fragment}
```{r}
map_dbl(l, 1)
```

:::

::::

## Type Match {.smaller}

You'll get an error for coercion

```{r}
#| error: true
map_chr(l, "x")
```

. . .

You have to explicitly coerce

```{r}
map_chr(l, ~as.character(.x$x))
```

. . .

You'll get an error if element doesn't exist

```{r no-element}
#| error: true
map_chr(l, "z")
```

. . .

Unless you set a default value

```{r no-element-default-val, error = TRUE}
map_chr(l, "z", .default = NA_character_)
```

## Quick note: missing values {.smaller}

* In the prior case, specifying `NA` would work, instead of `NA_character_`
* Generally, I think it's better to specify the type
* **General programming rule:** The more strict the better 
* Because (base) `R` likes to be inconsistent, here are the `NA` types

. . .

| Type | NA value|
|:-----|:-------:|
| character | `NA_character_` |
| integer   | `NA_integer_` |
| double    | `NA_real_` |
| logical   | `NA` (see [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19564748/why-is-there-no-na-logical)) |

. . .

```{r log-na}
typeof(NA)
```

## More quick examples

Please copy the code below so you have it locally

```{r }
df_list <- list(
  data.frame(var1 = 1:5),
  data.frame(var1 = 1:3),
  data.frame(var1 = 1)
)
```

## Compute `mean(var1)`

For each data frame. You try first!

`r countdown::countdown(4)`

## Answer

```{r }
map(df_list, ~mean(.x$var1))
```

. . .

Return a vector

```{r }
map_dbl(df_list, ~mean(.x$var1))
```

## Some more examples

[**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

```{r economics}
econ <- economics |>
	mutate(year = lubridate::year(date))
econ
```

## Let's fit a simple model to each year

*Caveats*

* We'll discuss a more elegant way to do this later
* This is not (statistically) the best way to approach this problem
* It's a good illustration, and in my experience there are lots of times where
this approach works well, even if this particular example is a bit artificial

## split by `year`

```{r by_year}
by_year <- split(econ, econ$year)
str(by_year)
```

## Model

What is the relation between personal consumption expenditures (`pce`) and the unemployment percentage over time?

. . .

**Problem:** We don't have the percentage. Let's compute!

## You try first!

`r countdown::countdown(4)`

. . .

```{r perc}
by_year <- map(by_year, 
               ~mutate(.x, percent = unemploy / pop))
str(by_year)
```

. . .

Could also do

```{r}
by_year <- map(by_year,
               ~.x |> 
                mutate(percent = unemploy / pop))
```


## Fit the models

Fit a model of the form `lm(percent ~ pce)` to each year

**You try first!**

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

## Fit the models

. . .

```{r many-models}
mods <- map(by_year, 
            ~lm(percent ~ pce, data = .x)
            )
str(mods)
```

## Extract coefficients

### You try first

**Hint**: use `coef()`. For example, try `coef(mods[[1]])`

`r countdown::countdown(2)`

## Solution

. . .

```{r coefs}
coefs <- map(mods, coef)
coefs[c(1:2, length(coefs))]
```

## Extract slopes

AKA - the coefficient that is not the intercept

**You try first**

`r countdown::countdown(2)`

## Extract slopes

. . .

```{r slopes}
slopes <- map_dbl(coefs, 2)
slopes
```

## Plot

```{r plot-slopes}
#| message: false
relation <- tibble(year = names(slopes),
                   slope = slopes)

ggplot(relation, aes(slope)) +
	geom_histogram(fill = "dodgerblue", color = "white")
```

# List Column

## Piping & List Column

We could also have done the previous in a pipeline with a list column

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "1-3|4-5|6-7|8|9"
#| output-location: fragment
mods_econ <- econ |> 
  group_by(year) |> 
  nest() |> # : split(econ, econ$year)
  mutate(data = map(data,
                    ~mutate(.x, percent = unemploy / pop)), # compute 'percent'
         mods = map(data, 
                   ~lm(percent ~ pce, data = .x)), # fit the model
         coefs = map(mods, coef), # extract coefficients
         slopes = map_dbl(coefs, 2) # extract slopes
         )
mods_econ  
```

## What do our friends think about this...

## What do our friends think about this...

![](https://c.tenor.com/GnURUkDiJOQAAAAC/lakers-fans.gif){width=900px}

## Let's plot it

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
mods_econ |> 
  ggplot(aes(slopes)) +
	geom_histogram(fill = "dodgerblue", color = "white")
```

# More Practice 

If we have time before the lab

## Practice

* Compute the standard deviation of every `mtcars` column
* Copy and run the following code to obtain 50 bootstrap samples
* Fit the following model to each bootstrap sample:
    + `mpg ~ disp`
* Extract $R^2$ and plot the distribution

```{r bootstrap}
bootstrap <- function(df) {
  df[sample(nrow(df), replace = TRUE), , drop = FALSE]
}
```

# Next time

## Before next class

- Readings
    + [{fs}](https://tidyverse.org/blog/2018/01/fs-1.0.0/)
    + [AdvR(2e) 9.4-9.6](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/functionals.html#map-variants)
- Lab 2

# Lab 2
